papers.title: The origin of high velocity stars from Gaia and LAMOST
papers.author_names: Cuihua Du Hefan Li Heidi Jo Newberg Yuqin Chen Jianrong Shi Zhenyu Wu Jun Ma

abstract.text: Based on the second Gaia data (Gaia DR2) and spectroscopy from the LAMOST Data Release 5, we defined the high-velocity (HiVel) stars sample as those stars with $v_{\\\\mathrm{gc}} > 0.85 v_{\\\\mathrm{esc}}$, and derived the final sample of 24 HiVel stars with stellar astrometric parameters and radial velocities. Most of the HiVel stars are metal-poor and $\\\\alpha$-enhanced. In order to further explore the origin of these HiVel stars, we traced the backwards orbits of each HiVel star in the Galactic potential to derive probability parameters which are used to classify these HiVel stars. Of these, 5 stars are from the tidal debris of disrupted dwarf galaxy and 19 stars are runaway-star candidates which originate from the stellar disk.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The authors identify 24 high-velocity (HiVel) stars from Gaia DR2 and LAMOST DR5 data, defining HiVel stars as those with $v_{\mathrm{gc}} > 0.85v_{\mathrm{esc}}$. Most are metal-poor and α-enhanced, with 5 classified as tidal debris from disrupted dwarf galaxies and 19 as runaway stars originating from the Galactic disk via dynamical ejection or binary supernova mechanisms. Orbital backward integration in the Galactic potential provides probability-based classification, leveraging precise astrometry and spectroscopy to distinguish between disk-origin and accreted halo origins.

source_fields.title: The origin of high velocity stars from Gaia and LAMOST

source_fields.sections_text: 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel classification and dynamical analysis of high-velocity stars that distinguishes between hypervelocity, runaway, and fast halo stars, addressing the problem of accurately identifying their origins and providing new insights into Galactic dynamics and stellar ejection mechanisms. 4. Conclusions and summary: Our analysis of 24 HiVel stars reveals their diverse origins, including tidal debris from dwarf galaxies, disk runaways, and thick disk-like stars, with metallicity and orbital characteristics providing key insights into their galactic origins. 2. Data and target selection: This section describes the selection of high-velocity (HiVel) stars by cross-matching Gaia and LAMOST data, applying criteria for signal-to-noise and radial velocity consistency to identify a reliable sample for further analysis. 3. Chemical abundances and orbits of HiVel stars: The chemical abundances and orbital properties of HiVel stars indicate a mix of origins, including the disk, bulge, and dwarf galaxy accretion, with their [$\\alpha$/Fe] and kinematic data supporting diverse formation histories and ejection mechanisms.

abstract: Based on the second Gaia data (Gaia DR2) and spectroscopy from the LAMOST Data Release 5, we defined the high-velocity (HiVel) stars sample as those stars with $v_{\\\\mathrm{gc}} > 0.85 v_{\\\\mathrm{
esc}}$, and derived the final sample of 24 HiVel stars with stellar astrometric parameters and radial velocities.
Most of the HiVel stars are metal-poor and $\\\\alpha$-enhanced.
Of these, 5 stars are from the tidal debris of disrupted dwarf galaxy and 19 stars are runaway-star candidates which originate from the stellar disk.
title: The origin of high velocity stars from Gaia and LAMOST

catalog_assessment.evidence: Abstract explicitly states they derived a final sample of 24 HiVel stars using Gaia DR2 and LAMOST DR5 data, with astrometric parameters and radial velocities. They present a candidate list and perform orbital analysis.

papers.title: Faint, high proper motion star candidates selected from the SDSS and PS1 catalogs

abstract.text: High proper motion stars probe several extreme stellar populations, including nearby objects, Galactic halo stars, and hyper-velocity stars. Extending the search for high proper motion stars, to faint limits can increase their numbers and help to identify interesting targets. We conduct a search for faint (r>19.5 mag) high proper motion stars (>~200 mas/yr) by comparing the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) - Data Release (DR) 10 catalog to the Pan-STARRS1-DR1 stacked image catalog. Our main selection criterion is stars that moved >1.5 arcsec and up to 7 arcsec between the SDSS and PS1 epochs. We identify 2923 high proper motion stars, of which 826 do not have proper motion in the GAIA-DR2 catalog and 565 are not listed in the GAIA-DR2 catalog. Our SDSS-PS1 proper motions are consistent with the GAIA-measured proper motions with a robust rms of about 10 mas/yr.

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The experiment identified 2923 faint high proper motion star candidates, with 826 lacking GAIA-DR2 proper motion measurements, revealing non-random proper motion structures and color deviations indicating unreliable photometry in faint sources, primarily among $H \\geq 24$ mag objects. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel search for faint, high proper motion stars with motions exceeding 1″.5 between SDSS and PS1 epochs, addressing the gap in identifying such objects for follow-up studies of high-velocity stars, cool objects, and stellar remnants. Search for proper motion stars: A multi-step astrometric and photometric selection process using SDSS, PS1, and Gaia data identified 4486 candidate high-proper-motion stars, with false positives systematically removed through rigorous image inspection and consistency checks. The high proper motion candidates: This section identifies 2923 high proper motion star candidates from SDSS and PS1 data, cross-matches them with GAIA-DR2, and validates their proper motions with good agreement between SDSS-PS1 and GAIA measurements, while assessing measurement uncertainties.

abstract: High proper motion stars probe several extreme stellar populations, including nearby objects, Galactic halo stars, and hyper-velocity stars.
Extending the search for high proper motion stars, to faint limits can increase their numbers and help to identify interesting targets.

We conduct a search for faint (r>19.5 mag) high proper motion stars (>~200 mas/yr) by comparing the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) - Data Release (DR) 10 catalog to the Pan-STARRS1-DR1 stacked image catalog
Our main selection criterion is stars that moved >1.5 arcsec and up to 7 arcsec between the SDSS and PS1 epochs.
We identify 2923 high proper motion stars, of which 826 do not have proper motion in the GAIA-DR2 catalog and 565 are not listed in the GAIA-DR2 catalog.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract describe selection of 2923 faint high proper motion stars from SDSS and PS1 catalogs, specifically mentioning these probe hyper-velocity stars. They present a new catalog with proper motion measurements and compare to Gaia DR2.
